ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Milo Ice (iced Singaporean malt chocolate drink). FOODON_03311737 (chocolate malt beverage) is the closest defensible match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKV (chocolate-flavoured drink) used as closest defensible match for Milo Ice; no exact FoodEx2 entry for Milo Ice exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Milo Ice or equivalent iced malt chocolate beverage.

main_ingredients: Milo powder (malted barley, cocoa, sugar, skimmed milk powder, vitamins and minerals)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of Milo Ice (approximately 300 g) is the typical serving size at Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops (kopitiams), often prepared with Milo powder, ice, and sweetened condensed milk.

nutrient_source.title: Milo (powder) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Milo powder/drink as reported per 100 g. The iced preparation (Milo Ice) uses the same Milo powder base; the per-100g nutrient profile reflects the powder-based drink composition as recorded in HPB. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values were cross-referenced with the Nestlé Milo product label (USDA Branded Food equivalent) given that HPB reports these as 0 or trace; values of 1.56 g saturated fat, 0.07 g trans fat, and 0.0 mg vitamin C per 100 g are retained from the input record with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Milo Ice as a prepared iced drink. GI of 55 is estimated based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Milo powder drink (chocolate malt beverage, GI ~55). Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale, GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (low: ≤55; medium: 56–69; high: ≥70). The iced preparation does not materially alter the glycaemic response of the dissolved sugars. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (93 g/100 g × 300 g serving = 279 g) / 100 = 153.45, rounded to 153.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (72.7 g/100 g) and significant sodium (530 mg/100 g); sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Milo Ice entirely due to its very high sugar load (72.7 g/100 g), which independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, compounded by 530 mg sodium/100 g from the powder; request Milo Kosong over ice with low-fat milk and no condensed milk as a lower-risk alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high sugar (72.7 g/100 g) and carbohydrate (93 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre, producing rapid glycaemic excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Milo Ice is contraindicated for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to 72.7 g sugar/100 g and 93 g carbohydrate/100 g with zero fibre, causing rapid blood glucose spikes; the iced format does not reduce glycaemic impact, and the addition of sweetened condensed milk further elevates the sugar load — switch to unsweetened iced cocoa with low-fat milk and no added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar intake linked to elevated triglycerides, dyslipidaemia, and increased CVD risk; saturated fat (1.56 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.07 g/100 g) present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Milo Ice due to its very high sugar content (72.7 g/100 g) which promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and dyslipidaemia, combined with saturated fat (1.56 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.07 g/100 g) that adversely affect LDL cholesterol; limit to occasional consumption at most and prefer unsweetened alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content (72.7 g/100 g) drives pro-inflammatory mediator production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Milo Ice as the very high sugar content (72.7 g/100 g) promotes systemic inflammation and may exacerbate RA flares; replace with unsweetened iced green tea or plain iced water with lemon.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Milo Ice (Malay: Milo Ais; Chinese: 美禄冰) is a popular cold beverage widely consumed in Singapore, Malaysia, and across Southeast Asia. It is prepared by dissolving Nestlé Milo powder — a malted barley and cocoa-based drink mix fortified with vitamins and minerals — in cold water or milk, then serving it over crushed ice or ice cubes. In Singaporean hawker centres and kopitiams (coffee shops), Milo Ice is commonly prepared with a generous scoop of Milo powder, ice, and often sweetened condensed milk, resulting in a rich, sweet, chocolatey drink with a distinctive malt flavour. The texture is cold and refreshing, with a thick, slightly grainy mouthfeel when extra Milo powder is heaped on top. Nutritionally, Milo Ice is energy-dense at 372 kcal/100 g, with an extremely high sugar content of 72.7 g/100 g and 93 g carbohydrate/100 g, making it a high-glycaemic load beverage. It contains virtually no protein (0 g/100 g) or fat (0 g/100 g) from the powder base alone, though the addition of condensed milk adds saturated fat and additional sugar. The powder is notably high in calcium (909 mg/100 g), magnesium (218 mg/100 g), and iron (16.4 mg/100 g) due to fortification. Sodium is significant at 530 mg/100 g. Clinically, Milo Ice is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its very high sugar load, moderate sodium, and pro-inflammatory profile. It is halal-certified and vegetarian-suitable.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Milo Ice?
Is Milo Ice suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of Milo Ice?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95 per input record), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values retained from input with medium confidence (0.7) as cross-referenced from product labelling. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for chocolate malt beverage.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — Milo Ice (powder-based drink) contains no protein contribution at the per-100g powder level as reported by HPB; this reflects the powder composition without added milk. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — the Milo powder base contains no fat as reported by HPB at this formulation level; saturated fat (1.56 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.07 g/100 g) are present from the full product formulation per product label data. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per product label data. potassium_mg reported as 0.0 per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this entry. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Milo powder/drink as of 2026. GI category corrected to 'low' (GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category per University of Sydney GI Database classification: low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Note: the per-100g values reflect the Milo powder-based drink composition as recorded in HPB; actual nutrient content of a prepared Milo Ice serving will vary depending on the ratio of powder to water/milk and the addition of sweetened condensed milk. Two portions are recorded: 1 standard glass (300 g) and 1 upsized cup (450 g), both sourced from GrabFood platform data (medium confidence). Glycemic load per serving calculated using 300 g standard glass: GL = 55 × (93/100 × 300) / 100 = 55 × 279 / 100 = 153.45, reported as 153.5.

Updated glycemic_load_per_serving from 154.7 to 153.5 to reflect accurate calculation: 55 × (93/100 × 300) / 100 = 153.45, rounded to 153.5.
